[[RunningHead]]

Tracing a Memory

K. Blacker M.D.

K. Blacker

K. H. Blacker, M.D.
2315 Stockton Boulevard
Sacramento, California 95817

K. Blacker  

SUMMARY

Comparisons of the nests of associations within which a single memory reappeared during the course of an analysis deemed successful by analyst and patient revealed changes in content and structure of these matrices. Judges, given a sample of associations surrounding a reappearance, were able to relate a particular appearance to its sequential location in the development and flow of an analysis.

In the study samples, the patient became increasingly active and insightful as the analysis progressed. Resistance decreased, and the memory, initially given as a narrative report, became a powerful metaphor for the examination of conflicts concerning aggression and its defense, passivity. The results of the judges' analyses and of the thematic analysis suggest that a close examination of associations surrounding a repeated stimulus, a single memory, can be used as an index of analytic progress.

[[RunningHead]]

Early Ego Development and the Déjà Vu

Bernard Pacella M.D.

Bernard Pacella

Bernard L. Pacella, M.D.
The Roosevelt Hospital
428 West 59 Street
New York, New York 10019

Bernard Pacella  

SUMMARY

It is proposed that certain déjà vu experiences may be the result of a sudden partial decathexis of the current situation, and a concomitant recathexis of primitive experiences which

316

readily allow certain symbolic representations to force themselves into conscious awareness as a discrete and circumscribed experience in the perceptual ego, carrying with it only a strong sense of the familiar rather than discrete memories. The drive investment that cathects the current situation or object to be "familiarized" (déjà vu) may be derived in substantial part from the "face-gestalt configuration" of the symbiotic and, later, nonsymbiotic mother, described as an early crucial organizer of the psyche.

Certain déjà vu experiences may be initiated by anxiety (often castration anxiety) induced by the current reality situation. They could but not necessarily be dynamically similar or related structurally to other déjà phenomena.

It is speculated that there may be certain dynamic parallels between the déjà vu, the dream screen of Lewin, and the Isakower phenomenon.

Reflections on the Special Senses in Relation to the Development of Affect with Special Emphasis on Blindness

H. Blank M.D.

H. Blank

H. Robert Blank, M.D.
53 Winslow Road
White Plains
New York 10606

H. Blank  

SUMMARY

In congenital blindness, the absence of vision can be compensated for by the utilization of the tactile and auditory senses, resulting in normal affect and ego development. Some of the major problems in the way of such compensation were noted. Adventitious blindness produces disruption of ego functions, with severe affect manifestations, notably grief and mourning reactions and actual dependency. The factors favoring successful or unsuccessful resolution of mourning and restoration of normal ego functions were indicated.

Discussion of Bernard L. Pacella's Paper "Early Ego Development and the Déjà Vu"

Margaret Mahler M.D.

Margaret Mahler

Margaret S. Mahler, M.D.
10 West 66 Street
New York, New York 10023

Margaret Mahler  

AN ATTEMPT TO DISCUSS the rich details of Dr. Pacella's paper has led me to undertake a research project in its own right, to present the results of which now certainly would go far beyond the scope of a short discussion.

Taking Dr. Arlow's classical paper, "The Structure of Déjà Vu Experience," as his point of departure, Dr. Pacella has attempted to apply the open-ended dimensions of presentday psychoanalytic metapsychology to deduce from these relatively rarely discussed déjà mechanisms, the material that lies beyond the basic or primal repression barrier, at which most psychoanalytic writers have thus far stopped their clinical and theoretical investigations.

To me, efforts to go beyond that line of primal repression are courageous undertakings, which are promising, indeed fascinating, and without which I believe psychoanalytic research would become self-limiting. Those efforts constitute a long overdue challenge to rethink what Freud left for us to work out. With Dr. Pacella, I think that the fabric of déjà phenomena might derive from a point far behind the oedipal situation, from a time when images were primary and words secondary.

Submitted May 31, 1974

Psychoanalytic Research: Three Approaches to the Experimental Study of Subliminal Processes (Psychological Issues, Monograph 30)

Edited by Martin Mayman. New York: International Universities Press, vii + 135 pp., $7.00.

Roy Whitman M.D.

Roy Whitman

Roy M. Whitman, M.D.
University of Cincinnati Medical Center

Roy Whitman  

This is the thirtieth monograph in an illustrious series, the first of which was Identity and the Life Cycle by Erikson, and which boasts such outstanding contributions as Rapaport's The Structure of Psychoanalytic Theory and Gill's Topography and Systems. Such works command careful and repeated study and are used by many institutes in their courses on theory. This monograph also merits careful attention: it is good to know how "they" are thinking.

"They" are that gifted group of psychoanalytic psychologists, many of whom began at Menninger's with Rapaport and have now scattered to universities throughout the nation. They not only contribute to the psychoanalytic literature and are intimately conversant with psychoanalytic theory, but they comprise an impressive group in American psychology, respected even in academic circles, where most analysts, Freud among them, are considered passé.

Almost all the authors fit this pattern, Mayman, the editor, being a classic example. He received his Ph.D. at the University of Kansas, after having spent three years studying clinical psychology with David Rapaport at The Menninger Foundation. He graduated from the Topeka Institute for Psychoanalysis in 1960.

[[RunningHead]]

Book Notices

SISYPHUS OR THE LIMITS OF EDUCATION. By Siegfried Bernfeld. Translated by Frederic Lilge. Berkeley, Los Angeles, London: University of California Press, 1973, xxx + 120 pp., $6.75.

Bernfeld's monograph of 1925 has hitherto been unavailable in English; its translation by a Professor of Education bears witness to the impact of psychoanalysis on educational theory. In a brief Foreword, Anna Freud stresses that this analytic contribution to education is potentially much greater today than it was half a century ago. A more extensive Preface by Professor Peter Paret, Bernfeld's stepson, provides biographical information about the author and places the book in its historical context. In order to grasp Bernfeld's intent in this work, the failure of the residential school he had founded in 1919 as well as his passionate commitment to psychoanalysis, Marxism, and Zionism must be understood.

Sisyphus began with the assumption that the educational system was urgently in need of reform, in spite of the lack of adequate theoretical understanding upon which to base this necessary change. Hence, the theoretical limits of the power of education constituted the subject of Bernfeld's inquiry. He was not interested in the empirical technology of instruction, but only in the underlying educational philosophy. This had generally developed without much actual experience in working with children, having been based instead on individual preconceptions about the psychology of childhood and utopian schemes of ethical reform.

Bernfeld defined education broadly as the process whereby children are acculturated. He saw the function of the schools as only one of the means to that end. The modern school system is the product of an eighteenth-century pedagogical revolution initiated by Rousseau and Pestalozzi, that is, it developed in the context of the radical social changes of our era.

WHEN WE CONSIDER THE RESULTS of our analytic interventions, the essential question is whether there has been a proper resolution of the basic conflict; whether it has been either modified or decathected for a variety of reasons or dictated by external events.

Clinically, Phyllis presented many unusual features. She resembled the Wolf Man's older sister (Freud, 1918).

[[RunningHead]]

Poems in Persons

Ernest Wolf M.D.

Ernest Wolf

Ernest Wolf  

By Norman N. Holland. New York: W. W. Norton, 1973, 183 pp., $6.95.

Professor Holland, best known in our field for Psychoanalysis and Shakespeare, here examines the relationships among literary works, readers, and writers. Specifically, he focuses on lyric poetry and one particular fantasy: the unconscious wish to undo one's separateness from a nurturing other.

Holland studied the writer H. D. as revealed in her book Tribute to Freud and in selected portions of her poetry. He extracts something unitary and characteristic about the writer, which, after Yeats, he calls a "myth." He relates the myth to style and to character in the psychoanalytic sense. Like the "identity theme" conceptualized by Lichtenstein, the myth reveals the organic unity of the psyche. On the basis of his empathic response to H. D.'s poetry, Holland defines her myth: "to close up the gap between inner and outer, spiritual and physical, male and female, by perfect, timeless signs which she can be and be in." Both psychopathology and creative writing may act out the same underlying myth; both will "close the gap with signs."

Holland next describes the reactions of students to literary works. Based on anamnestic data and the results of projective tests on these subjects, he concludes that each reader re-creates the work of art in his own individual style. He then tries to explore, "How do different readers build a bridge from one reading to another so as to form a community of shared experiences which keep, even so, their very private status?

Metapsychological Analysis of a Parapraxis

Harvey Kelman M.D.

Harvey Kelman

Harvey Kelman, M.D.
1212 Edgevale Road,
Silver Spring, Maryland 20910

Harvey Kelman  

AS ANNA FREUD (1969) HAS RECENTLY POINTED OUT, metapsychology as "… theory building, distant from the area of clinical material… has become the bugbear of the clinically oriented analyst who feels wholly divorced from it. This brings about a division which, in the long run, threatens both areas with sterility: the theoretical field, due to the absence of clinical data; the clinical field, due to a diminution in their theoretical evaluation and exploration. What is lost, finally, is what used to be considered as a sine qua non in psychoanalysis: the essential unity between clinical and theoretical thinking" (p. 153).

That unity is the result of a fruitful amalgam. Clinical observation breathes life into abstract metapsychological constructs. Likewise, application of the five coordinates of metapsychology provides explanatory breadth for even the most familiar clinical data. This paper will illustrate the inter-relatedness of clinical observation and metapsychological application in the analysis of a simple parapraxis.

1

The patient was a 40-year-old school teacher who had entered analysis because of sexual frigidity. One day she reported an error which occurred during registration of a new student.

Submitted September 21, 1974

A MAJOR DIFFICULTY in the description of the course of a psychoanalysis is the absence of a comprehensive, commonly accepted framework that can be used to set in order the diverse data that is being accumulated during the course of the work. This has made it difficult for analysts to compare their results and to make meaningful longitudinal studies.

Anna Freud (1969) has highlighted the importance of looking at psychoanalytic data from both the metapsychological and clinical viewpoints. The metapsychological constructs, she asserted, foster a multidimensional approach that helps organize diverse aspects of complex clinical phenomena and permits the full meaning of the phenomena to emerge. Kohut (1970) further delineated this position:

[[RunningHead]]

On the Technique of Child and Adolescent Analysis

Isidor Bernstein M.D.

Isidor Bernstein

Isidor Bernstein, M.D.
12 Welwyn Road
Great Neck, New York 11021

Isidor Bernstein  

DURING THE COURSE OF ITS GROWTH to its present status, child analysis began, as children often do, to follow in the footsteps of its parents. Like the analysis of adults, analysis of children originated as a form of therapy, became a method of research, and contributed toward a general theory of personality. Particularly with regard to research, child analysis has furnished its progenitor with important additions to analytic knowledge concerning object relations and ego development in infancy and early childhood. Child analysis also became a means of clarifying, enriching, refining, and validating or disproving formulations and hypotheses of analysis that had been based on reconstructions from the treatment of adults. The special focus of child analysis has been on development of the personality.

Anna Freud: THE WRITINGS OF ANNA FREUD, VOLUME VII, 1966-1970. PROBLEMS OF PSYCHOANALYTIC TRAINING, DIAGNOSIS, AND THE TECHNIQUE OF THERAPY. New York: International Universities Press, 1971.

Elisabeth R. Geleerd, ed.: THE CHILD ANALYST AT WORK. New York: International Universities Press, 1967.

Gerald H.J. Pearson, ed.: A HANDBOOK OF CHILD PSYCHOANALYSIS. New York: Basic Books, 1968.

Victor Smirnoff: THE SCOPE OF CHILD ANALYSIS. New York: International Universities Press, 1971.

Benjamin B. Wolman, ed.: HANDBOOD OF CHILD PSYCHOANALYSIS. New York: Van Nostrand Reinhold, 1972.

[[RunningHead]]

The Sleepy Analyst: Some Observations on States of Consciousness in the Analyst at Work

James McLaughlin M.D.

James McLaughlin

James T. McLaughlin, M.D.
121 University Place
Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania 15213

James McLaughlin  

SUMMARY

The freely hovering attentiveness of the analyst at work is part of a continuum of consciousness ranging between vigilance and sleep. In this paper I have made an attempt to align the phenomena of sleepiness and sleeping in the analyst within this continuum, and to cite observations and impressions that can throw light upon these as both states of nonspecific narcissistic depletion and specific countertransference phenomena involving sympathetic and reciprocal identifications of the analyst with and toward his patient. Attention to these phenomena is clinically rewarding and potentially enhancing to our understanding of the working of the analyzing instrument.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Development of Paternal Identity: A Critical Review of the Literature on Nurturance and Generativity in Boys and Men

John Ross Ph.D.

John Ross

John Munder Ross, Ph.D.
35 West 92nd Street
New York, New York 10025

John Ross  

SUMMARY

I have reviewed psychoanalytic contributions on nurturance in boys and men—issues related to the problem of male sexuality in general. Freud emphasized the "negative" oedipal aspects of wishes to bear and rear babies, their regressive and passive implications. Successive generations of analysts have discovered the active, progressive dimensions of procreative strivings. They have underscored womb and breast envy, envy of maternal power; identification with the mother as a means of recreating the lost relationship with her and of building one's own ego identity; the need for a developmental perspective in pursuing the dramatic changes in a boy's procreative, productive, and caring attitudes and desires. I have closed by sketching such a scheme, hypothesizing successive stages in the epigenesis of paternal identity. The thrust of this review is to demonstrate, first, that "maternal" or, better, parental aspirations are organic, indeed organizing components in the evolution of male identity and, second, that there exists a substantial, though neglected history of psychoanalytic thought to this effect.

[[RunningHead]]

Separation-Individuation and Object Constancy

John McDevitt M.D.

John McDevitt

John B. McDevitt, M.D.
55 East 87 Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

John McDevitt  

THE TERM OBJECT CONSTANCY WAS INTRODUCED into the psychoanalytic literature by Hartmann in 1952, when he said, "…there is a long way from the object that exists only as long as it is need satisfying to that form of satisfactory object relations that includes object constancy" (p. 163). Since that time most psychoanalytic writers on the subject have agreed with Hartmann that object constancy should be regarded as one stage in the development of object relations; that it refers to both the libidinal and the cognitive aspects of the young child's attachment to the mother; and that it probably presupposes, on the side of the ego, a certain degree of neutralization of aggressive as well as of libidinal energy (Hartmann, 1952), (1953). There has been considerable disagreement among psychoanalysts, however, about the definition, the timing, and the criteria for evaluating this stage of development.

Most writers have placed the attainment of "libidinal" object constancy within an age range of six to eighteen months (for a thorough review of the literature, see Fraiberg, 1969); (see also Décarie, 1965)

Presented in a shortened version at the New York Psychoanalytic Society, January 1972.

The Master's Children Center, New York, N.Y.

Supported by NIMH Grant MH-08238, USPHS, Bethesda, Md.; FFRP Grant G69-458, Foundation's Fund for Research in Psychiatry, New Haven, Conn.; and The Master's Children Center, New York, N.Y.; Margaret S. Mahler. Principal Investigator, John B. McDevitt, Co-Principal Investigator.

Submitted February 21, 1975

[[RunningHead]]

On the Current Status of the Infantile Neurosis

Margaret Mahler M.D.

Margaret Mahler

Margaret S. Mahler, M.D.
10 West 66 Street
New York, New York 10023

Margaret Mahler  

AS ANNA FREUD (1936), (1962), (1965) expressed it—and as Ritvo (1974) and Lebovici (1973) have said—our current view on the concept of infantile neurosis differs considerably from that which prevailed at the time of Little Hans and the Wolf Man.

I would like to briefly discuss how the concepts of infantile neurosis, of object relations, and of narcissism might be clarified by including in their consideration the symbiotic and separation-individuation phases of human development. I believe if these considerations were to become integrated into our thinking in terms of assessing the widening scope of neurotic and other disturbances of childhood (cf. also Nagera, 1966), it might help us to comprehend such issues as the parallel existence, the simultaneous development, and the interrelationships of what we call object relationship and narcissism—or transference neurosis and narcissistic disturbances—and also borderline symptomatology.

Dr. Mahler is Director of Research at the Masters Children's Center; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Albert Einstein College of Medicine; Faculty Member of the New York and Philadelphia Psychoanalytic Institutes.

This discussion was given as part of the panel, "The Current Status of the Infantile Neurosis" at the Annual Meeting of the Association for Child Psychoanalysis on July 22, 1973 in Paris, France.

Submitted June 5, 1974

[[RunningHead]]

Some Observations on Object Constancy in the Psychoanalysis of Adults

Joan Fleming M.D.

Joan Fleming

Joan Fleming, M.D.
Department of Psychiatry C270 University of Colorado Medical Center
4200 East 9th Avenue
Denver, Colorado 80220

Joan Fleming  

SUMMARY

In conclusion, I would like to restate the focus of this paper by calling it a study in the metabolism of experience from two angles: (1) the integrative task confronting the ego in organizing drive and object interactions to assist the ego in its regulatory functioning; and (2) a timetable of progression in accomplishing that task. Evidence was presented from the psychoanalytic treatment of an adult man who suffered object

757

deprivation during the "hatching out" subphase of separation-individuation. In the course of therapy, an effective object constancy was reconstituted and the process of individuation of self and Other was re-established. The therapeutic work provided the patient with experiences in transactions between self and object useful for reorganizing the trauma of early object deprivation and thereby mobilized his resources for a resumption of the developmental process distorted in childhood.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Parenthood as a Developmental Phase

Henri Parens M.D.

Henri Parens

Henri Parens, M.D.
Eastern Pennsylvania Psychiatric Institute, Children's Unit
Henry Avenue & Abbottsford Road
Philadelphia, Pa. 19129

Henri Parens  

Dr. Irwin Marcus introduced the Panel members, noting that Therese Benedek, in a presentation to this Association in 1958, parented the title and theme of this Panel. Marcus set the question of a developmental phase of parenthood in an epigenetic context. He observed that all our basic psychoanalytic concepts, from their inception by Freud, are developmental. Beyond the Oedipus complex, Freud detailed a puberty and climacterium. Of the subsequent contributions in expansion of Freud's original formulation, Marcus could touch on the work of only a few. Spitz refers to phases of development in terms of critical periods and organizers. His theories explicate Freud's theory of the complemental series and of fixation, and generate implications for prevention and therapy. Mahler's formulations of the normal autistic, symbiotic, and separation-individuation phases of development have stimulated a series of panels on "The Experience of Separation-Individuation in Infancy and its Reverberations Through the Course of Life." Moving another step ahead on the continuum into adolescence, Blos has described four developmental tasks of that phase of development: the second individuation, achievement of ego continuity, integration of a sexual identity, and mastery of residual trauma.

Marcus noted that progressive and regressive aspects of developmental phenomena occur throughout the life cycle. The achievement and maintenance of an adult ego identity cannot be regarded as a permanent, unalterable state. Zetzel considered it a basic fact of life that every important maturational challenge presents significant regressive threats. She believed that those with limited symptomatology during a developmental crisis have greater potential for genuine maturity than those who have consolidated regressive defenses in their definitive character structure. Marcus saw in this postulate the suggestion that those with residual partial failures in the resolution of their earlier intrapsychic conflicts have an opportunity for new and better solutions.

responseSet.title: On Reconstruction
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Reconstruction

Phyllis Greenacre M.D.

Phyllis Greenacre

Phyllis Greenacre, M.D.
501 East 87 Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

Phyllis Greenacre  

MY PRIMARY AIM IN THIS PAPER is to discuss the functions and significance of reconstruction in our psychoanalytic work. I shall attempt to describe the process of reconstruction in the course of psychoanalytic therapy, the principles of its methodology rather than details of technique, something of the process that takes place in the work of reconstruction, and to give some brief clinical illustrations. I will then present my ideas regarding the contribution that careful reconstructive work can make to individual therapy as well as to clinical research, which in turn may gradually influence and expand our psychoanalytic theory. It is especially helpful in bringing to light the impact of disturbances in infancy on later oedipal and postoedipal developments.

The idea of reconstruction seems to me a fundamental one in the practice and theory of psychoanalysis. The terms construction and reconstruction are familiar to most analysts. They appear in the current literature with less frequency, however, than they did 37 years ago, when Freud's article on "Constructions in Analysis" was published (1937). Even then Freud noted that the term "reconstruction" seemed often to be replaced inaccurately by "interpretation." Certainly the two words have different connotations.

This paper was presented as the Fenichel Lecture of the Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Society on January 24, 1975.

Submitted April 4, 1975

693

[[RunningHead]]

Incompleteness in "Successful" Psychoanalyses: A Follow-Up Study

Jerome Oremland M.D. , K. Blacker M.D. and Haskell Norman M.D.

Haskell Norman

Jerome D. Oremland, M.D.
K. H. Blacker, M.D.
Haskell F. Norman, M.D.
2299 Post Street, Suite 311
San Francisco, California 94115

Haskell Norman  

SUMMARY

In the course of studying various aspects of patients' responses to and experiences following analysis, two cases who had had "successful" analyses, when called back two years following termination for follow-up interviews, provided unusually clear information about incompleteness. In one case, an aspect of reality joined a transference resistance as a specific kind of "reality resistance." In the other case, the continuing partially analyzed quest to live out the transference fantasy was described by the patient as it was experienced during the analysis and "maintained" postanalytically. At termination, the postanalytic course, the subsequent self-analytic attempts, and the eventual manner in which each presented himself for further treatment are evaluated in terms of the specific kind of incompleteness.

[[RunningHead]]

The Interpretations of the Past: Childhood Trauma, Psychical Reality, and Historical Truth

J. Schimek Ph.D.

J. Schimek

J. G. Schimek, Ph.D.
Yale University School of Medicine
333 Cedar Street
New Haven, Connecticut 06510

J. Schimek  

SUMMARY

Even Freud's initial "seduction" theory deals with the past as reconstructed by the analyst, not as remembered by the patient. The changes in the belief in the factual reality of infantile traumas reflect the vicissitudes of "the great father theme" in Freud's self-analysis and clinical thought, as well as his lasting commitment to a certain model of causal explanation. The concept of a psychical reality created by instinctual drives was never a sufficient substitute for actual events. Aspects of human development that Freud considered basic and universal, such as the Oedipus complex, had to be grounded on prehistoric deeds, transmitted as universal inherited memories and phylogenetic "schemata."

Is it still possible or even necessary to assume that interpretations, like archaeology, aim at the recreation of the past as it once existed? Or that the creation of new meanings and relationships, mediated by different levels of symbolic and linguistic processes, can be reduced to a causal explanation on the model of natural science? Such questions point to the need for a new look at the actual process of interpretation, its underlying rationale and function. Freud's ideas about universal schemata and the search for actual deeds or action patterns—as the source of later ideational structures—can be related to some aspects of modern developmental psychology (e.g., Piaget) and structuralist approaches.

Psychoanalysis as an Art and the Fantasy Character of the Psychoanalytic Situation

Hans Loewald M.D.

Hans Loewald

Hans W. Loewald, M.D.
65 Trumbull Street
New Haven, Connecticut 06510

Hans Loewald  

WHAT FOLLOWS DOES NOT claim to measure up to the standards of scientific rigor or conceptual precision. In the fluidity and ambiguity of thought and style, this presentation is perhaps all too much affected by its subject matter.

Most of us, whether engaged solely in psychoanalytic practice or engaged also in psychoanalytic research and theory-building, stress the scientific aspects and potentialities of our discipline. Freud did so, and he rejected the suggestion that there might be something nonscientific or unscientific, something resembling art, about psychoanalysis. In the recent past there has been much emphasis on psychoanalysis as a basic science, or as the foundation for or as part of a general psychology. It has been claimed that this constitutes the lasting value of psychoanalysis, and doubts have been expressed, even within the ranks of psychoanalysts themselves, with regard to its viability as a form of psychotherapy. I myself have no doubt about its therapeutic value and potentialities, although I do question whether we can expect general or ready recognition of its therapeutic worth and effects—given the anti-individualistic tendencies and simplistic behavior-modification trends in our culture.

This paper was delivered as the Rado Lecture at the Columbia University Psychoanalytic Clinic for Training and Research on May 21, 1974.

Submitted July 5, 1974

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Development of the Sense of Self

Joseph Lichtenberg M.D.

Joseph Lichtenberg

Joseph D. Lichtenberg, M.D.
1616 18th Street, N.W.
Washington, D.C. 20009

Joseph Lichtenberg  

SUMMARY

I have presented an account of the development of the sense of self from infancy through early childhood. I approach the psychoanalytic concept of the "self" by maintaining a strict distinction between an experiential realm, in which the sense of self can be understood empathically, and the nonexperiential realm, which refers to functioning of the psychic structures id, ego, and superego. In this way, the sense of self can be seen as arising during the infantile stage as islands of experience that then, bit by bit, are formed into more ordered groupings of images. Bodily self-images, self-images in relation with objects seen as distinctly separate, and images of the grandiose self associated with idealized self-objects become blended experientially into a sense of self that has the quality of cohesion—of unity and continuity in time, space, and state. By tracing the way cohesion occurs and the effect cohesion of the sense of self has on development in both the experiential and nonexperiential realms, I hope to indicate the position the sense of self occupies as a significant basic concept in psychoanalysis.

SUMMARY

Certain transformations of narcissism and idealization contribute to the creative process. A consideration of some forms and transformations of narcissism discussed by Kohut is offered here as applied to the Romanticism of the Tristan-and-Iseult

422

legend, with specific reference to a revival of early ego states. Andreas-Salomé's views on the dual orientation of narcissism are presented as a particularly helpful way of reconciling the apparent contradiction between the "new" and "old" views of narcissism.

[[RunningHead]]

Pour Une Psychoanalyse De L'Art Et De La Créativité

John Gedo M.D.

John Gedo

John Gedo  

By Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel. Paris: Payot, 1971, 262 pp., F. 25.70.

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel, a leading figure in the Paris Psychoanalytic Society, has collected her papers on applied analysis in this volume and has added a striking Introduction in which she takes up the issue of the chronic resistance on the part of many psychoanalysts to extratherapeutic applications of depth psychology. She proposes that this reluctance to reach psychoanalytic conclusions about every facet of human behavior, generally rationalized on the grounds of scientific rigor, is often a way of perpetuating a split in the analyst's mind: on one side—that of the treatment of patients—he functions in accord with reason and psychoanalytic knowledge; on the other—in various aspects of his mental life—he remains free to adhere to some system of illusion, whether it be a religious dogma or, as is more likely in contemporary France, a political ideology. The author believes these illusions to be equivalent to infantile fetishes.

The psychoanalytic viewpoint of this book is uncompromising. It may be characteristic of our French colleagues that the theoretical framework takes the libido concept more seriously and more concretely than current American writings tend to do. The bibliography is conspicuously poor in American references (in startling contrast to the practice of British and German authors), but leans to some degree on the work of Melanie Klein. Adherence to this school is neither dogmatic nor unthinking; in fact, one of these papers consists in a demonstration that the Kleinian hypothesis about creativity is inadequate: creative work is not always a matter of making reparation to the object, but may have to do with affirming the cohesion and continuity of the self.

The best of the other papers examines the film Last Year at Marienbad. It succeeds in correlating many aspects of style with the psychological content of the story viewed in terms of object relations. Although this analysis is by no means exhaustive, it is cogent and methodologically sound.

Psychoanalysis—Science and Research: Twin Studies as a Paradigm

Edward Joseph M.D.

Edward Joseph

Edward D. Joseph, M.D.
Mt. Sinai School of Medicine
11 East 100 Street
New York, New York 10029

Edward Joseph  

ONE OF THE MANY COMPLAINTS made of psychoanalysis is that it is not scientific, but is based upon the findings of one man whose followers accept his word blindly, applying his ideas without attempts at validation, verification, and corroboration in ways acceptable to scientists.

In the comments that follow, I shall examine some of the concepts involved in the current understanding of the term science to see whether psychoanalysis should be included under the heading of science and I shall also discuss some problems of psychoanalytic research and examine studies of twins made by psychoanalysts and others, as a model of research.

Science and Research

To one engaged in a scientific field, science can be considered a body of facts or truths, systematically arranged and showing the operation of certain ideas, principles, or possible laws. A scientific attitude can then be said to be characterized by a reluctance to accept a statement as being true unless its validity is demonstrated and its relationship to pre-existing knowledge shown.

A revised version of the Plenary Address given before the American Psychoanalytic Association, December, 1973.

Submitted December 1973

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Termination: Problems and Techniques

William Robbins M.D.

William Robbins

William S. Robbins, M.D.
10 Presidential Blvd. No.
Suite 200-B
Bala Cynwyd, Pa. 19004

William Robbins  

Sylvan Keiser in his introductory remarks described termination as a process characterized by unique dynamics which pose theoretical and technical problems requiring further study and understanding. Some psychoanalysts believe there are dynamics that are characteristic for the termination phase and that require an alteration in the classical psychoanalytic technique at termination. Keiser suggested that perhaps the major technical problems of the period rest with the analyst's ability to maintain his usual analytic posture when confronted by the special demands created by the introduction of the question of termination.

Keiser believed that termination and the terminal phase cannot be studied as isolated phenomena, but must be considered in relation to the personality types involved in the analytic process. While themes exist that are common to all analytic relationships, in termination the variations on these themes are numerous and are influenced by the idiosyncratic disposition of both participants.

Turning to the task of defining criteria for a successful termination, Keiser expressed his view that termination could be considered a naturally occurring analytic development in those circumstances where the patient's description of his real life was consonant with the observable psychoanalytic data. When the analytic process reveals that structural changes do not parallel the patient's report of his improved external life, or where the analytic observation indicates great structural change without a corresponding report of improvement in the patient's real life, it becomes extremely difficult for the analyst to decide whether termination is appropriate. Under such circumstances, the analyst is confronted with the need to decide if the patient's decision for termination reflects a mature judgement or is an expression of resistance.

A Critical Assessment of the Future of Psychoanalysis a View from Within

Ira Miller M.D.

Ira Miller

Ira Miller, M.D.
1718 Hermitage Road
Ann Arbor, Michigan 48104

Ira Miller  

The Chairman, Irving Sternschein introduced the proceedings by pointing out that this is the first of a two-panel series on the subject. At the second panel several psychoanalytically sophisticated scholars and thinkers from a number of other disciplines will share with us their critical appraisal as they view us from without.

Sternschein wondered why our topic so often appears on meeting programs and in our scientific journals. The more analysts seem to know, the more they know they don't know. In March, 1910, when Freud addressed the second Psychoanalytical Congress in Nuremberg on "The Future Prospects of Psychoanalytic Therapy," he must have been prompted by concerns about the results of therapy and, therefore, about the durability of psychoanalysis. Freud encouragingly predicted a substantial improvement in therapeutic effectiveness and over-all gains for society. He saw the improvement as coming from three directions: First, from accrued knowledge about the id; secondly, he seemed to have anticipated the increased role that was later to be attributed to ego functions in human development and in the therapeutic process; from a third direction, he looked forward to a "general prophylactic effect: of the dissemination of psychoanalytic knowledge on society as a whole."

Some of the most salient psychoanalytic motives and/or consequences of past self-examination by analysts in connection with the future of psychoanalysis have been related to the nature and conditions of the analyst's work. Good scientific papers have a quasi-interpretive impact on their audience. Papers and discussions involving reassessment of our science, such as will be heard on this panel, usually have a similar interpretive effect.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Denver, May 1974.

Submitted June 26, 1974

[[RunningHead]]

Some Pitfalls in the Assessment of Analyzability in a Psychoanalytic Clinic

Howard Huxster M.D. , Richard Lower M.D. and Philip Escoll M.D.

Philip Escoll

Howard Huxster, M.D.
Richard Lower, M.D.
Philip Escoll, M.D.
111 North 49th Street
Philadelphia, Pa. 19139

Philip Escoll  

SUMMARY

We have described some of the problems encountered in an analyzability committee's evolving approach to the assessment of applicants to a psychoanalytic clinic. A primary error was to think in terms of the level of drive development—equating pathology at an oedipal level with analyzability. Recognition that the more important issue for assessment purposes was the presence or absence of those ego capacities and personality characteristics necessary for success in analysis has led to a reorientation of our approach.

[[RunningHead]]

"A Critical Assessment of the Future of Psychoanalysis: A View from the Outside"

Ira Miller M.D.

Ira Miller

Ira Miller, M.D.
1718 Hermitage Road
Ann Arbor, Michigan 48104

Ira Miller  

The chairman, Martin Wangh, commented on the decreasing popularity of psychoanalysis since after the Second World War. The widening scope of psychoanalysis, based on increased knowledge, was accompanied by greater demands for psychiatric services that led to community psychiatry and the various peripheral forms of psychoanalytically-oriented treatment. The earlier idealization of psychoanalysis was followed by disillusionment and condemnation. In addition, the ever-changing social scene brought with it serious challenges to psychoanalysis. In the first panel of this series, "A Critical Assessment of the Future of Psychoanalysis: A View From Within," our colleagues voiced their distress over the pessimistic mood expressed by both K. R. Eissler (1969)1 and Anna Freud (1969).2 It is a challenge to psychoanalysis to try to understand and help the distress of the young that has led them to an attitude that is antihistorical, anti-intellectual and anti-introspective at a time when analysts themselves contribute to the apocalyptic gloom that has enveloped the Western World.

After Ira Miller presented a brief summary of the first panel of this series, John O. Wisdom, University Professor of Philosophy and Social Science, York University, Toronto, spoke.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York City, December, 1974.

Submitted March 1, 1975

[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of Dr. Pacella's Paper "Early Ego Development and the Déjà Vu"

Phyllis Greenacre M.D.

Phyllis Greenacre

Phyllis Greenacre, M.D.
501 East 87 Street
New York, New York 10028

Phyllis Greenacre  

WHEREAS I ENJOYED READING this paper, it made me realize how little I have thought about the déjà vu experience. Certain areas that have been discussed do, however, overlap with the problems of early development which have preoccupied me, and to which Dr. Pacella adds new dimensions.

I am glad to have his specific description of the shift from dependence on body contact perception to the utilization of visual perception as a leading, organizing agency in the very incipience of object relatedness. It seems to me that there is an almost continuous interplay between the two. Dr. Pacella evokes the picture of the activity of the infant during the first month, alternately seizing the nipple with the mouth and then losing it again as the sphincter-like sucking action of the mouth relaxes. This seems to be the forerunner of a somewhat similar pattern emerging in the focusing and unfocusing of the eyes. It is followed in a later stage by peek-a-boo and the other lose-and-find-again games that are used to establish familiarity and begin reality testing. Vision permits a kind of attachment to the mother, especially to her face, with the focused eyes, while relaxation permits a more distant vision, probably at first blurred and infiltrated with the intake through the other senses as well.

Submitted May 31, 1974

[[RunningHead]]

Group Psychology and the Structural Theory: A Revised Psychoanalytic Model of Group Psychology

Stephen Saravay M.D.

Stephen Saravay

Stephen M. Saravay, M.D.
Long Island Jewish-Hillside Medical Center Long Island Jewish Division
New Hyde Park, New York 11040

Stephen Saravay  

SUMMARY

It has been my aim in this paper to revise the existing psychoanalytic theory of group psychology in accordance with current

87

structural concepts. The need for fundamental revision in the existing theory of group organization is demonstrated by its restriction to an oedipal paradigm, which cannot account for the regression to an oral paradigm of group organization during group formation.

Freud's explanation of regression in crowds is reviewed. The limitations inherent in Freud's topographic and narcissistic models are demonstrated; irreconcilable contradictions are shown to exist between the two theories. A structural model of group psychology that is free from internal contradictions and provides a unifying explanation for both regression and merging in the crowd is developed. As a consequence of these revisions it is possible to conceptualize preoedipal organizations of group structure in addition to the oedipal paradigm proposed by Freud.

SUMMARY

The assessment of a completed analytic case and its follow-up is presented to illuminate the nature of the analytic process with regard to psychic conflict and its resolution. The clinical material demonstrates a repetitive pattern of conflicts and the development of a self-analytic function as the significant outcome of the analysis.

100 York Street

Richard Isay  

SUMMARY

I have attempted to refocus attention on the influence of the observation of parental intercourse by a child between the ages of five and a half and eight on his sexual behavior and mental life during early adolescence. The material was reassembled and reconstructed from the analysis of an adult patient. Attention to reconstructions and reassemblages of early adolescent experiences from adult analyses may contribute to our understanding of this developmental stage.

[[RunningHead]]

Time and Affect in Psychopathology

Peter Hartocollis M.D.

Peter Hartocollis

Peter Hartocollis, M.D.
C. F. Menninger Memorial Hospital
Topeka, Kansas 66601

Peter Hartocollis  

SUMMARY

The less an individual is conscious of time, the more likely he is to be free of psychopathology. Under normal circumstances, time remains implicit and largely unconscious; but it tends to dominate consciousness in schizophrenia and borderline conditions as well as in normal people under stress. This exaggerated awareness of the sense of time is experienced as an affectless state. When consciousness becomes dominated by affect, the sense of time speeds up and then recedes, until all awareness of time is lost. Distortions in the sense of time reflect defensive operations with reference to unconscious fantasies that are characteristic of one's internal object relationships. Depending on the effectiveness of the defense in blocking the destructive intent and allowing for the fulfillment of the libidinal yearnings of such fantasies, time distortions tend to be experienced as pleasant (creative, blissful, ecstatic) or painful (boring, frightening, depressive).

[[RunningHead]]

On Vegetarianism

Stanley Friedman M.D.

Stanley Friedman

Stanley Friedman, M.D.
9 East 96th Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

Stanley Friedman  

SUMMARY

Two analytic cases with at least some degree of vegetarianism were presented and their oral cannibalistic impulses and conflicts were noted. The lack of full understanding of

405

the ultimate etiology of vegetarianism was discussed, especially because defenses against cannibalism can only be described as a necessary but insufficient precondition for this phenomenon. The prominence of contradictory identifications and concrete and literally understood introjective mechanisms was noted as possibly reinforcing the prohibition against eating meat. Speculations were made relating vegetarianism to vivid oral primal-scene impressions, and to its possible expression within the oral triad.

[[RunningHead]]

National Conference on Psychoanalytic Education and Research

Robert Emde M.D.

Robert Emde

Robert Emde  

Herbert S. Gaskill, the Chairman, traced the development of the Conference on Psychoanalytic Education and Research. The stated goal was to reassess and give perspective to our field, to look to the future, and to update the previous overview of Lewin and Ross.1 Nine general areas involving special concerns of the Association were identified, and a preparatory commission assigned to each of these areas. They consisted of: I. The Tripartite System of Psychoanalytic Education; II. The Ideal Institute; III. Age and the Psychoanalytic Career; IV. The Relationship of Psychoanalysis to Universities; V. The Relationship of Psychoanalysis to Current Changes in Medical and Psychiatric Education; VI. Psychoanalytic Education and the Allied Disciplines; VII. Psychoanalytic Research; VIII. The Relationship of Psychoanalysis to Social and Community Issues; and IX. Child Analysis.

These commissions, consisting of a Chairperson and Recorder and fourteen to seventeen members, worked independently over a two-year period, often with subcommittee reports and multiple position papers, before the eventual preparatory commission reports were written.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York City, December 14, 1974, Chairman: Herbert S. Gaskill, M.D.

1Lewin, B. D. and Ross, H. (1960), Psychoanalytic Education in the United States. New York: Norton.569

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis in Community Psychiatry: Reflections on Some Theoretical Implications

Ralph Wadeson M.D.

Ralph Wadeson

Ralph W. Wadeson, Jr., M.D.
2141 K Street, N. W.
Washington, D. C. 20037

Ralph Wadeson  

Dr. Viola W. Bernard, Chairman of the Panel and of the Committee that prepared it, opened the session. She explained that this Panel, presented by members of the Association's Committee on Community Psychiatry, reflects the stage reached in a process that evolved over the five and a half years of the Committee's existence. She thought it would be of interest, therefore, to review some of the factors that gave rise to the Committee, how it has sought to carry out its charge, and some of the steps by which it arrived at focusing today on certain theoretical implications from community psychiatry for psychoanalysis.

Although the Association's interest in social and group phenomena is by no means recent, the development of community and social psychiatry has certainly accelerated and intensified within the past two decades, gaining its greatest momentum in the nineteen-sixties. Furthermore, the push for changes in mental health service delivery, itself a sociopolitical issue, occurred in the dynamic context of social ferment with which it became inevitably enmeshed: the civil rights movement, the "war on poverty," the black protest movement, student unrest, and Vietnam with all its psychosocial repercussions. Even though much of the pioneering in community psychiatry was done by psychoanalysts, unfortunately, these two approaches to psychological well-being, one focused on minutia within the individual mind, the other directed toward population groups, became polarized, with consequent mutual misperceptions and derogation. The establishment in 1968 by this Association of the Standing Committee on Community Psychiatry expressed a counterthrust to such an adversary situation, by community-oriented analysts, to bring the concepts and practices of both into synergistic relationship. Although surveys show that a majority of analysts work in a range of non-office settings, and in multiple roles, many of them apparently dissociated such work as incompatible with their sense of identity as an analyst.

[[RunningHead]]

The Young Adolescent: Clinical Studies

By Peter Blos. New York: The Free Press, 1970, xxiii + 252 pages, $8.95.

Jules Weiss M.D.

Jules Weiss

Jules M. Weiss, M.D.
San Francisco, Cal.

Jules Weiss  

Peter Blos's many contributions to the psychoanalytic literature on child development have been soundly reasoned and well grounded in clinical material. In this monograph, the clinical material, derived from the supervised psychotherapy of two young adolescents and their parents, is used to highlight a detailed discussion of developmental theory as it has been advanced in Blos's earlier works, to add certain perspectives on the role of aggression and to set forth some principles of psychotherapy. The clinical material has been integrated with theory. The extensive presentation of clinical material is in itself a significant contribution, especially insofar as knowledge of the first stage of adolescence has been limited both by difficulties in recall during adult analyses and by the problems inherent in treating these youngsters. Of the many issues Blos raises, this review will touch on only a few: What contributes to the forward movement in therapy? What factors are related to regression in preadolescence? How do problems related to narcissism and depression begin to crystallize during this phase of development?

In recent discussions of the therapeutic process, an increased emphasis has been placed upon the role of the ego in regulating the emergence of new material in the therapeutic situation. Blos uses the case of Susan to illustrate how the increased capacity of the ego to cope with emerging material permits the patient to actively bring this material into the therapeutic situation where she can master it and thus overcome serious impediments in the way of her development. The therapist's task is often one of deciding whether to interpret a resistance or to wait out the child's internal progress and thrust forward. He must keep in mind that periods of apparent standstill may not call for active interpretation of resistance, since the situation may be one of ego consolidation.

[[RunningHead]]

Selected Problems of Adolescence: With Special Emphasis on Group Formation

Monograph Series of The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, No. 3. By Helene Deutsch. New York: International Universities Press, 1967, 136 pp., $6.00

Eugene Kaplan M.D.

Eugene Kaplan

Eugene H. Kaplan, M.D.
Great Neck, New York

Eugene Kaplan  

Following the death of her beloved husband in 1964, Helene Deutsch gave up her practice and withdrew from activities at the Boston Psychoanalytic Institute. It was not a retreat into permanent solitary inactivity, but a prelude to a redirection of cathexis. This monograph on adolescence was the outcome of her courageous adaptive response to the anguish of mourning. "A stream of teenagers started coming to my house regularly, enticed by their friends' reports that there was a strange woman in Cambridge to whom you could go and talk about anything you pleased."

Helene Deutsch has combined the distillate of a half-century of psychoanalytic experience with an indomitable self-renewal through empathy and avowed identification with adolescents. This volume seems to reflect her unflinching awareness that time is running out. It is like a second draft, in need of further revisions, elaborations, and reorganization.

At the same time, the volume's unfinished quality symbolizes poetically for me the author's marvelous relationship to us, the younger generations. For her gift of wisdom and insights must be worked over to make it our own. Here, wide-ranging considerations of adolescence are not cast in definitive form for uncritical acceptance. Her propositions require evaluation, refinement, and systematization, but they nevertheless improve our conceptualizations about adolescence, and much of the psychoanalytic literature since 1967 reflects their stimulating heuristic.

The phase's basic themes, issues, and conflicts are inherent and unchanging, and Deutsch reviews them with the experience of three generations: the conflict between generations then and now, the importance of sublimation, the reverberations of regressively reactivated narcissism, empathy and its link to identification, and the reactions to sexual maturation. She also underscores adolescence as but one phase of an evolving developmental process.

[[RunningHead]]

On Normal Adolescence

By The Committee on Adolescence, Group for the Advancement of Psychiatry. New York: Scribner, 1968, 127 pp., $3.95.

Morton Shane M.D.

Morton Shane

Morton Shane, M.D.
Los Angeles, Cal.

Morton Shane  

A steady appreciation of adolescence as a significant arena for conflict and structure building has been slow in developing. Freud's startling discoveries surrounding infantile sexuality had the effect of relegating the impact of the adolescent phase to a recrudescence of oedipal and preoedipal conflicts. Although in his Three Essays he clearly explicated the monumental changes required of the developing

246

individual in response to puberty for the achievement of genital primacy, in most of his writing Freud does not portray adolescence as specifically pathogenic for adult pathology.

For the most part, such underestimation of the pathogenic significance of adolescence has persisted in analytic literature, perhaps because of a phenomenon noted by Anna Freud in 1958:1 generally, the period of adolescence is not fully recaptured in the transference experiences of adult analytic patients. The full affective recall of phase-appropriate, overwhelming, and profoundly moving adolescent experiences is not achieved. Concomitant phase-appropriate defenses of intellectualization and repression and isolation of affect have been cited as contributing to this failure to recapture adolescent emotional states. Perhaps just because these powerful feelings are not recaptured, their significance can be ignored in the analysis, and in the literature as well.

In cases where the adult patient does succeed in attaining a revivication of his adolescence in the transference, the analyst is hard pressed to deal with the patient's profound aggressive and derisive attitudes.2 Resultant countertransference avoidance can lead the analyst to unconsciously shy away from making a reconstruction of his patient's experience.

Still another factor might contribute to the slighting of adolescence as a significant phase for character formation: the prevalence of the reductionistic tendency in psychoanalytic thinking that what is experienced earliest in life must therefore have the most profound effect.

[[RunningHead]]

Perception, Motives, and Personality

By George Klein, Ph.D. New York: Alfred A. Knopf, 1970, 464 pp., $9.50.

Arnold Goldberg M.D.

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold I. Goldberg, M.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Arnold Goldberg  

At one time or another philosophers, scientists, and most other curious people have wondered about the nature of the world out there. Not so much about whether it really exists, for most of us have settled that question, but rather—of what is it really made? The position of a somewhat denigrated group called naïve realists was that the world is truly composed of tables, chairs, red apples, people, etc. Bertrand Russell long ago said that naïve realism slowly led us to physics, which proved naïve realism was wrong. The world of electrons and the stuff of subatomic particles bears no resemblance at all to the red apples that we thought we knew; we are forced to believe in different worlds to fit the occasion or to have two minds—one for subjective experience and one for fundamental objective reality.1 Philosophers have by no means settled on the nature of the world, but only reluctantly have they appreciated the fact that a scientific investigation of what was felt to be a purely epistemological problem was in order.

1The most elegant and articulate statement of the two orders of the sensory world is given by Friedrich Hayek in The Sensory Order (University of Chicago Press, 1952, p. 7). He says: "It is the existence of an order of sensory qualities and not a reproduction of qualities existing outside the perceiving mind which is the basic problem raised by all mental events. Psychology must concern itself, in other words, with those aspects of what we naïvely regard as the external world which find no place in the account of that world which the physical sciences give us."643

[[RunningHead]]

A Psychoanalytic Model of Attention and Learning [psychological Issues, Monograph 23]

By Fred Schwartz and Peter H. Schiller. New York: International Universities Press, 1970, 134 pp., $7.50.

Mardi Horowitz

Mardi Horowitz

Mardi J. Horowitz
University of California, San Francisco

Mardi Horowitz  

With the publication of this volume, a historical task has been completed. David Rapaport, in a series of brilliant essays, attempted to cull a systematic theory of cognition from the existing psychoanalytic literature. This effort was part of a mission to make psychoanalysis a general psychology or, perhaps more precisely, to bring together the dual streams of clinical and experimental investigation under a central umbrella of theory. He had hoped to organize a series of postulates into a model of the mind and then test this model by experimentation. The "final model" would then be a product both of clinical studies through the psychoanalytic method of treatment and exploration, and experimental studies using the precision of design, statistical analysis, and controlled variation possible only in the laboratory. Fred Schwartz and Peter Schiller worked further on his basic postulates concerning consciousness, attention, and learning. They evolved designs amenable to laboratory study, and they conducted experiments. This monograph reports their results and their re-evaluation of the basic postulates.

When academic psychology was formalized as a discipline during the nineteenth century, the study of thought processes was a natural topic and the center of considerable interest. This early interest seemed to diverge into two streams. One could be called "medical psychology," the other "formal psychology.

[[RunningHead]]

The History of the Concept of Association of Ideas

John Gedo M.D.

John Gedo

John Gedo  

By David Rapaport. New York: International Universities Press, 1974, xiii + 189 pp., $8.50.

Publication of David Rapaport's 1938 doctoral dissertation will strengthen his reputation as a historian of ideas. This is a brilliant survey of the prehistory of psychology as a discipline, i.e., of the epistemological advances of the 17th and 18th centuries which made possible the scientific study of the human mind. Rapaport's central concern throughout his professional life was with the psychology of thinking, a concern that began with the focus of this work on the concept of association.

The history of this concept is investigated from three points of view: that of its development as modern philosophy picked up the threads of Hellenistic thought about the issues of being and consciousness, that of the formulation of "psychic law" or the unity of psychology, and that of the genesis of the current concept of association. The philosophers whose views on these matters are explicated in detail are Bacon, Descartes, Hobbes, Spinoza, Locke, Leibniz, Berkeley, Hume, and Kant.

In the early 17th century, associations were regarded as mere sources of errors in thinking; Descartes, who believed the sources of knowledge to be innate ideas, tried to specify the mechanism of associations in order to limit their undesirable influence. By contrast, Hobbes views associations as the determiners of mental processes, albeit with certain exceptions.

667

[[RunningHead]]

Scientific Thought and Social Reality: Essays by Michael Polanyi (Psychological Issues Monograph 32)

John Gedo M.D.

John Gedo

John Gedo  

Edited by Fred Schwartz. New York: International Universities Press, 1974, 158 pp. $8.00.

This superb collection is the last, posthumous fruit of George Klein's stewardship of Psychological Issues. Michael Polanyi came to the philosophy of science from physical chemistry, and these writings allude to psychoanalysis only tangentially. Polanyi's brilliant, historically informed defense of science as a social institution nevertheless offers the intellectual tools needed to affirm the legitimacy of introspection and empathy as the principal observational method of a scientific psychology.

As a contribution to the history of ideas, Polanyi's work is a stunning attack on logical positivism. A beautifully argued analysis of the evolution of scientific method demonstrates that induction is not the only pathway to truth; the tendency of scientists to minimize the role of speculation in their discoveries is irrational. Hume had already shown that no theory can be proven inductively; the ability to develop abstractions from observational data is only given to genius. The history of science has been less concerned with the process of discovery than with its justification. Discoveries are actually based on belief, self-confidence, and the power of grandeur, profundity, beauty, or elegance, rather than on passionless, pseudo-objective work.

These characteristics of science suggest that it can only be propagated by direct personal contact. Its tradition has been continuous from the days of Galileo through successors including Freud. The practice of science is thus an art, mastered by a tiny élite, and viable only if it can impress a wider public. Ultimately, attacks on science or outsiders' attempts to control it are like wars of religion. Such opposition has grown in the modern context which is characterized by barren skepticism about ideals and fervent reformist programs. In totalitarian societies, these utopian demands are clothed in pseudoscientific garb, and real science is subverted to utilitarian ends. Polanyi sees attempts to bend knowledge to practical uses, as if seeking the truth for its own sake were selfish, as disastrous in preventing scientific progress and in further depriving society of such desperately needed values as the love of truth.

John Gedo M.D.

John Gedo

John Gedo  

Edited by Robert Jay Lifton with Eric Olson. New York: Simon & Schuster, 1975, 372 pp., $9.95; paperback, $3.95.

This interesting volume contains a series of papers and discussions from the Wellfleet Conference where, for a number of years, an outstanding group of intellectuals have struggled to forge a new alloy, the discipline of psychohistory. Although many of the participants from the psychological side of the dialogue were not psychoanalysts, the theoretical framework of most of these contributions is that of psychoanalytic psychology. It is, in fact, this circumstance that lends coherence to this book, which is otherwise quite heterogeneous in its contents.

responseSet.title: The Fear of Looking
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Fear of Looking

David Terman M.D.

David Terman

David Terman  

By David W. Allen. Charlottesville: University Press of Virginia, 1974, x + 134 pp., $7.95.

Dr. Allen's thesis is that exhibitionism and scopophilia are very important components of both neurosis and human personality in general. He states, "Despite changes in psychoanalytic theory, the concepts of scopophilia and exhibitionism remain unchanged and little exploited." His monograph is an attempt to rectify the perceived omission.

The author's definition of the concepts is narrow. He relates the genetic roots of looking and showing to the pregenital sexual instincts. He reaches into biological theory to bolster his contention that such elements and conflicts over them have broad significance in early development and for later personality structure.

Allen then presents thirteen analytic cases to illustrate these factors in the patient's personality structure and their importance for the therapeutic process. He feels that the overcoming of the fear of showing oneself is a central factor in permitting resolution of the transference neurosis. This process is facilitated by the analyst's acceptance of what is revealed by the patient, by his own capacity to be unafraid of his curiosity about the patient, and by his willingness to show himself when it seems appropriate. Allen underlines the importance of the patient's experience of the analyst's perception of him—what he calls the analyst's "useful neutral involvement."

Finally, Allen discusses the part played by wishing to look and by the ability to see in a new way as components of creativity.

674

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Contemporary Science, Volume 3, 1974

John Gedo M.D.

John Gedo

John Gedo  

Edited by Leo Goldberger and Victor H. Rosen, New York: International Universities Press, 1974, xiii + 557 pp., $15.00.

The third volume of this annual of "integrative and interdisciplinary studies" upholds the high standards set by its predecessors. It even marks an advance, in defining a unique editorial policy with a sharper focus on intellectual problems and on issues amenable to empirical investigation.

This year, only two papers deal with topics in clinical psychoanalysis, and even these have a theoretical tone. The late V. H. Rosen develops a conceptual classification of the verbal interventions appropriate during psychoanalytic work; J. Schimek reexamines "the parapraxis specimen of psychoanalysis," Freud's Signorelli slip. A section on child development also stays close to clinical theory; it consists of M. S. Mahler's summary of her conclusions about the subphases of the separation-individuation process of early childhood and of F. Pine's note on the timing and functional significance of libidinal object constancy.

There are two sections devoted to empirical studies, one of them exclusively to sleep and dream research. The latter includes a balanced overview of this entire field by T. Anders and a more detailed review of the work of Richard Jones by C. Umbarger. It also presents two lengthy experimental reports. As part of a psycho-physiological study of nightmares and night terrors, C. Fisher, E. Kahn, A. Edwards, and D. Davis show, among other important findings, that certain night terrors are not dreams at all, but symptoms. H. Fiss, G. S. Klein, and E. Schollar report on a study of "dream intensification" as a result of prolonged interruption of REM periods, with a sophisticated discussion of the theoretical implications of this surprising finding. The other section dealing with quantitative investigations contains two papers. L. Luborsky and J. Mintz report on an elaborate study of the events precipitating episodes of momentary forgetting during analytic sessions. The results merely confirm familiar clinical propositions, but this work is a sample of an important and widely applicable research method. The second paper, L. Gottschalk's study of an analytic patient's hand-mouth approximations, correlates these with themes in his recorded associations.
